findings.description: The PDF uses a two-column layout for section headers and presenter names (e.g., 'Call to Order and Introductions' on left, 'Tammy Koger' on right). The HTML renders these as a single line or loses the distinct two-column alignment.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 shows 'Call to Order and Introductions' left-aligned and 'Tammy Koger' right-aligned in a clear two-column format. HTML page 1 shows them concatenated with an em dash: 'Call to Order and Introductions — Tammy Koger'.

findings.description: Section headers with presenter names use two-column layout in PDF but are collapsed to single line in HTML throughout all pages.
findings.evidence: PDF page 2: 'Workforce Solution' left, 'Mose Dorsey' right; 'GAC Happy Hour/Mentorship Program' left, 'Sam Westrich, Annette Lauber, Kathleen Wood' right. HTML renders these as single-line headers with em dashes.

findings.description: The title formatting differs between PDF and HTML. The PDF shows 'NCATP GRANT ADVISORY COUNCIL' with 'Meeting Minutes' and date in bold/italic centered text. The HTML renders the title with a horizontal rule underneath and different visual weight.
findings.evidence: PDF has all title lines centered in bold with italic date. HTML has the title as a large heading with a decorative horizontal rule, and date/time below in different styling.

findings.description: The 'Attendees:' label is bold in the PDF and appears as a distinct label. In the HTML it is rendered as bold but the overall formatting/indentation of the attendee list differs.
findings.evidence: PDF shows 'Attendees:' with indented names in a block. HTML shows 'Attendees:' bold followed by names in a continuous paragraph without the same indentation structure.

findings.description: Superscript formatting for ordinal numbers is lost in HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF shows '11-13th' with 'th' in superscript, '2nd Annual' with 'nd' in superscript. HTML renders these as plain text without superscript.

findings.description: Hyperlinks are visually present in the PDF as blue underlined text. In the HTML, the links appear to be rendered as plain text without the hyperlink styling.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 shows blue hyperlinks for URLs and link text. HTML appears to render some of these as plain text without visual link indicators.

findings.description: The 'Next GAC Meeting' announcement loses some bold formatting and the superscript on '8th'.
findings.evidence: PDF shows 'November 8th' with superscript 'th' and bold formatting. HTML renders it as 'November 8th 2023' without superscript.

findings.description: Heading hierarchy is broken. The document uses h1 for the title, then h2 for the subtitle, but all section headings are h3. The 'AT Expo' section heading has inconsistent bold styling within the h3 tag. More importantly, there are two <main> elements nested, which is a structural HTML issue.
findings.evidence: HTML has <main> inside <main>. All section headings use h3 uniformly, which is acceptable given h2 is used for subtitle, but the nested main elements are a structural problem.

findings.description: Links from the PDF are present as plain text in the HTML rather than as clickable hyperlinks. The PDF contained clickable hyperlinks for several URLs and email addresses, but the HTML renders them as plain text strings.
findings.evidence: PDF has clickable links like 'https://www.commerce.nc.gov/...', 'Christey.Kirk@dhhs.nc.gov', 'Julie.Dutchess@dhhs.nc.gov', etc. In the HTML these appear as plain text without <a> tags.

findings.description: The nested <main> element causes a minor structural duplication of the main content wrapper, though the actual text content is not duplicated.
